column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=webanno.custom.CharacterMention|CharacterName
#T_RL=webanno.custom.SocialRelationship|Direction|InclusionRelationType|Modality|RelationType|BT_webanno.custom.CharacterMention
#Text=Chapter 1
#Text=Steven Grant Rogers, better known as Captain America, succumbed to injuries sustained while fighting Thanos.
#Text=For three days, the best medical team in the world did everything they could to stabilize Rogers’ condition, but, in the end, there was nothing that could have been done.
#Text=When the Captain passed, he was at peace and surrounded by friends.
#Text=It was a good death.
#Text=That was the official story, anyway.
#Text=There were maybe a dozen people on earth (and a few in space) that knew the truth.
#Text=Steve Rogers, now known as Steve Carter, was currently enjoying retired life at Shady Oaks on the outskirts of Rochester - upstate New York.
#Text=As far as retirement homes went, Bucky had to admit this place was pretty nice.
#Text=There were a few large common areas - a library, a games room, and even a gym.
#Text=The grounds surrounding the building were small but well kept.
#Text=If you looked out from a North facing room on the fourth floor - like the one Steve had - you could see the murky blue of Lake Ontario disappear into the curve of the earth.
#Text=Quaint, but not dull.
#Text=The kind of place Steve deserved.
#Text=“What you thinking about, Buck?”
#Text=Bucky raised his eyebrows and turned from the window.
#Text=“I’m just wondering if you’re technically cheating when you go lawn bowling.
#Text=All these poor bastards probably can’t figure out why they can never beat you.”
#Text=Steve chuckled and shook his head.
#Text=“See - you don't even deny it.”
#Text=“I let them win sometimes.”
#Text=The corners of Steve’s mouth twitched into a smile.
#Text=“Sometimes I wish I could tell everyone what an absolute
#Text=shit
#Text=you are, Steve.”
#Text=“No one would believe you.”
#Text=“No, I guess not.”
#Text=Bucky shrugged.
#Text=“Can you imagine?
#Text=‘Hey - Captain America never actually matured past twelve years old’.
#Text=People would think I was running a smear campaign.”
#Text=“The last thing
#Text=you
#Text=need is more negative publicity.”
#Text=Steve’s grin faltered.
#Text=He paused for a moment.
#Text=“Steve?”
#Text=“I’m -” His words were cut off as a cough racked through his body.
#Text=Not a small one  - his body shuttered and the wet hacking noise cut across the room.
#Text=“Do you need me to get someone?
#Text=A nurse?”
#Text=Bucky stepped forward and placed his hand against Steve’s back.
#Text=He stopped coughing, but his breathing was still ragged.
#Text=“No,” Steve wheezed.
#Text=“I’m alright.
#Text=It’ll pass.”
#Text=He lifted a glass of water from the side table and sipped between breaths.
#Text=“Hey - hey.
#Text=Breath with me.”
#Text=Bucky crouched next to Steve’s chair.
#Text=“In - one, two, three, four.
#Text=Hold - one, two, three four.
#Text=Out - one, two, three, four.
#Text=Hold - one, two, three, four.”
#Text=They stayed locked in their breath exercise - the one Bucky learned when they were young, and Steve was always forgetting his medication - for a few minutes.
#Text=Bucky could’ve sworn it felt like hours.
#Text=“I’m good, Buck.
#Text=Really,” Steve finally said.
#Text=Bucky nodded once, curtly.
#Text=“I’ll know if you’re lying to me.
#Text=That nurse, Leanna, she’s sweet on me.
#Text=She’ll tell me if you’re not.”
#Text=Steve didn’t respond, but he did smile and pulled Bucky into a hug.
#Text=Bucky squeezed him back.
#Text=“Take care of yourself.
#Text=I mean it.”
#Text=“I will.
#Text=I will.”
#Text=Steve stood from his chair and walked to the door.
#Text=Bucky shouldered on his sweater, despite the August heat, and shoved his hand into his pocket to hide any flash of metal from his arm.
#Text=“I’ll be back next week.
#Text=Maybe even with Sam, depending on when he gets back from his mission.
#Text=Text me if you need anything.”
#Text=Steve nodded.
#Text=“Same goes for you.”
#Text=He glanced at his phone.
#Text=“I
#Text=do
#Text=still know how to use those things, even if the font’s a bit smaller than I remembered.”
#Text=Bucky chuckled as he walked out.
#Text=He kept his head down, carefully - there was a delicate balance of avoiding people’s gaze without making it look like he’s purposely avoiding people’s gaze.
#Text=Still, a few of the seniors throw him a glance.
#Text=Bucky suspected that was more to do with his long hair and scruff, though, rather than the fact he was an internationally wanted criminal at one point.
#Text=He signed the sign-out sheet in his best fake scrawl - James Carter.
#Text=Bucky even looped the ‘J’ all fancy.
#Text=“Good to see you again, James.”
#Text=Bucky looked up.
#Text=The brunette nurse, Leanna, smiled at him.
#Text=“Good to see you too,” he says politely.
#Text=He didn’t want to lead her on, but he also didn’t want to lose a place in her good books.
#Text=“Did you have a good visit with your uncle?”
#Text=“Great uncle, actually,” Bucky said.
#Text=He smiled to himself.
#Text=“But yeah, I did.
#Text=Keep an eye on him for me, will you?”
#Text=“Of course - see you next week?”
#Text=He nodded.
#Text=“As always.”
#Text=In the parking lot, his bike sat under the shade of a grand maple tree.
#Text=The drive back to the city was long, but Bucky didn’t mind.
#Text=Driving kept his mind clear - he could let his thoughts wash over him and push them to the back of his head.
#Text=The wind would push against his body.
#Text=He’d fix his eyes on the road, on the ever-coming roll of the asphalt, on the colours and makes of the passing cars, and on the shifting light of the sun.
#Text=He didn’t have to think about anything else.
#Text=He could ignore all the thoughts that constantly bit at the edges of his mind and just drive.
#Text=By the time he reached New York, the sun was sinking behind the spread of skyscrapers.
#Text=Warm gold light spread down the streets.
#Text=The shadows of the buildings grew and cast shade over the alleys, but it was not yet the dangerous blackened cover of night.
#Text=Bucky parked his bike in the lot under his Brooklyn apartment.
#Text=The building was only a few blocks from where he and Steve had grown up - if Bucky wanted he could be there in five minutes - but the borough was unrecognizable from the place he remembered.
#Text=The city was the same, more or less.
#Text=Sure, there were new buildings, but the old ones hadn’t disappeared.
#Text=New York City was a tapestry - a sleek modern building would sit sandwiched between a historic landmark and a bodega built in the 80s.
#Text=The people who walked the streets might’ve worn shorter shorts, brighter colours, and lower pants, but they were still New Yorkers.
#Text=Instead of burying their faces in newspapers, they buried their faces in their phones.
#Text=They took the subway; they worked too much; they never stopped.
#Text=New York was, as always, New York
#Text=Brooklyn, on the other hand, had changed.
#Text=Fundamentally.
#Text=It didn’t feel the same anymore - Bucky had thought settling at ‘home’ would make the transition back to the States easier, but now he wondered if it was the worse that way.
#Text=He could adjust to an entirely new place, but he could never stop seeing Brooklyn for what it once was.
#Text=The city, in Bucky’s head, existed doubly.
#Text=Every street was not a single street, but the overlay of the past and the present bundled in one.
#Text=When he thought about it, his head hurt almost as much as his heart.
#Text=Buck took the stairs to his third-floor apartment.
#Text=The glass elevator only made him feel the familiar vice of claustrophobia.
#Text=His neighbour from 3B - a young woman with pink hair and a nose ring - waved at Bucky.
#Text=He didn’t wave back.
#Text=Outside his unit, 3D, Bucky paused.
#Text=He could hear something, faintly, coming from inside.
#Text=He closed his eyes.
#Text=The floorboards creaked.
#Text=Whoever was inside had to be at least his size, if not larger.
#Text=In the air, the scent of an unknown scent lingered.
#Text=Not cologne - the intruder would have realized that would be too obvious - but a soap mixed with something else.
#Text=Mixed with gunpowder.
#Text=Bucky didn’t have anything on him - the seniors’ home generally frowned upon people bringing in firearms.
#Text=In any case, Bucky hadn’t anticipated trouble.
#Text=In the few months since Thanos, the world had grown quiet.
#Text=An aftershock of peace.
#Text=Now, he had to ready himself for a fight.
#Text=With his ear pressed to the door, Bucky listened to the movement in his unit.
#Text=The footsteps inside were purposefully light - not the kind of care that a petty thief would take.
#Text=He wrapped his hand against the doorknob and turned it, slowly.
#Text=It was still locked.
#Text=The break-in definitely wasn’t a random chance.
#Text=Someone had targetted Bucky and made sure to cover their tracks.
#Text=Bucky glanced, left and right, and made sure the hallway was empty.
#Text=He took a breath.
#Text=In - one, two, three, four.
#Text=Hold - one, two, three four.
#Text=Out - one, two, three, four.
#Text=Hold - one, two, three, four.
#Text=Bucky burst forward.
#Text=He slammed all his weight against the wood.
#Text=His apartment door exploded inward, cleaving free from the frame and the hinges.
#Text=Splinters of wood cascaded over the stone tile floor.
#Text=Bucky tensed his calf muscles and sprang toward the black-clad figure lounging on his couch.
#Text=The case in the man’s hand tumbled across the room and collided with the far wall.
#Text=Bucky’s head thundered with blood.
#Text=The world blurred with his movement and rage.
#Text=The man hit the ground, clearly unprepared for Bucky’s attack.
#Text=Still, the man rolled, sending Bucky flying into his glass coffee table.
#Text=He brushed the shards off his back and lunged, again, toward the man.
#Text=This time, he couldn't throw Bucky off.
#Text=Bucky’s arm, the metal one, tightened over the intruder's throat.
#Text=“Motherfucker.”
#Text=The intruder raised his hands over his head in surrender.
#Text=“Stand down, soldier.”
#Text=Bucky dropped his hand.
#Text=He blinked, trying to clear the rush of adrenaline.
#Text=“Shit.”
#Text=On the ground of Bucky’s apartment was Nick Fury.
#Text=He rubbed at the base of his throat.
#Text=Bucky reached down and pulled Fury to his feet.
#Text=“You could knock next time.”
#Text=Fury raised his eyebrow.
#Text=“Would you have answered?”
#Text=Bucky didn’t respond.
#Text=They both knew the answer.
#Text=“Why are you here?”
#Text=The lines of Fury’s face hardened again as he shifted his surprise behind his always-cool wall.
#Text=“I have an assignment for you.”
#Text=“Why not ask Sam?
#Text=He’s the one with the shield and stars and stripes.”
#Text=Fury crossed his arms over his chest.
#Text=“Wilson is a bit busy at the moment.
#Text=He doesn’t need another thing on his plate.”
#Text=He picked his dark briefcase up from where it had collided with the wall (leaving a dent, Bucky noted) and set it on the table.
#Text=Fury pressed his thumb against a glass pad, and the case clicked open.
#Text=“At any rate, Wilson wasn’t my first choice for this assignment.
#Text=You were.”
#Text=Bucky blinked.
#Text=“Me?”
#Text=From the case, Fury lifted free a plain looking file.
#Text=“You.”
#Text=He handed the file to Bucky.
#Text=Bucky opened the file.
#Text=Clipped to the stack of reports was a single, coloured picture of Spider-Man, suit on, mask off.
#Text=The kid's hair was a rumpled mess, and a wide grin was plastered across his face.
#Text=Bucky wasn’t sure what he expected, but it certainly wasn’t this.
#Text=“The kid?
#Text=I thought he was in Europe.
#Text=Dealing with that wannabe.”
#Text=“He was.”
#Text=Fury nodded.
#Text=“And that ‘wannabe’ is why we need to watch out.
#Text=The guy - called himself ‘Mysterio’ - created fake threats so he could take them down.”
#Text=“Like the firefighter-arsonist .”
#Text=“Exactly.
#Text=Wanted to make himself a hero by solving the problem he created.
#Text=Luckily for us, the kid saw through him.”
#Text=Fury sat at the kitchen table, and Bucky followed suit.
#Text=They didn’t have much longer to talk - one of his neighbours might’ve called the police.
#Text=If not, someone would notice the missing door.
#Text=“But this ‘Mysterio’ guy, he’s gone now, right?”
#Text=“He is.
#Text=But there’ll be others.
#Text=Barton’s retired - again.
#Text=Banner’s in no shape to fight — Thor’s off-world.
#Text=Rogers is out of the picture.
#Text=Stark and Natasha...”
#Text=Fury shook his head.
#Text=“My point is that there’s a power vacuum.
#Text=Things might be quiet right now, but they won’t always be that way.”
#Text=Bucky studied Fury’s face.
#Text=“I still don’t know exactly what you’re asking.”
#Text=Fury folded his hands into a bundle and met Bucky’s gaze.
#Text=“We’re not entirely powerless.
#Text=We have people capable of dealing with threats when they arise.
#Text=But - if possible - I’d like to avoid these threats from being created in the first place.”
#Text=“Aside from a select few, the world believes Rogers is dead,” Fury continued, “and even fewer still know that
#Text=you
#Text=were also injected with a form of the Super Soldier Serum.

#Text=That doesn’t mean anyone is going to stop trying to create a superhuman - without the deterrent of the Avengers, everyone is going to be crawling out of the woodwork to try and make the next Captain America.”
#Text=Bucky froze as the realization crawled over him.
#Text=“There’s only one obvious choice to copy.”
#Text=Fury nodded in confirmation.
#Text=“If anyone wanted to make the next version of the serum, they’re gonna target the kid.
#Text=He's only a few weeks shy of seventeen and there are hundreds of videos online of him lifting buses in pyjamas - that strength can’t be denied.
#Text=Bucky sighed and rubbed at his face.
#Text=The sunlight that flittered into his apartment was fading fast.
#Text=Outside, in the hall, he could hear a commotion.
#Text=He needed to leave.
#Text=“Is Hydra still out there?”
#Text=“Was it ever really gone?”
#Text=Bucky let out a breath through his teeth.
#Text=“Shit.”
#Text=Fury seemed to realize they needed to leave, too.
#Text=“Take care of the kid until his 18th birthday."
#Text="It's not your concern what happens to him after that."
#Text=He drummed his index finger on the file in Bucky’s hand.
#Text=“For now, watch him like a damn hawk.
#Text=Hydra won’t be the only one looking for him.”
#Text=Fury stood and took in the mess of Bucky’s apartment: the destroyed door; the shattered coffee table; the dent in the far wall.
#Text=Bucky shrugged.
#Text=“I was never crazy about this place.”
#Text=“We can help set you up.
#Text=If you agree to protect the kid, of course.
#Text=Just over a year.”
#Text=Bucky looked at the picture of the kid - his smile was so young and carefree.
#Text=Kid probably didn’t have a clue what kind of danger was lurking in the shadows.
#Text=He snapped the file shut and hardened his jaw.
#Text=“I’ll do it.”
#Text=Chapter 2
#Text=Fury, true to his word, had a new apartment ready for Bucky when he left the hotel at noon the next day.
#Text=The new place was simple.
#Text=The studio setup meant Bucky could see it all the moment he walked in: a simple queen bed, with a navy duvet; a desk and kitchen table in matching light wood; no decorations on the walls.
#Text=It could’ve been lifted straight out of a catalogue.
#Text=It probably
#Text=was
#Text=lifted out of a catalogue.
#Text=Bucky wasn’t about to complain though.
#Text=The apartment was functional.
#Text=That was all that mattered.
#Text=The building was about five blocks from where the kid lived.
#Text=Close enough that surveillance would be easy, but not too close that Bucky would risk an accidental encounter.
#Text=Bucky shrugged the backpack off his shoulder and pulled out the contents.
#Text=There were only a few things that had been worth saving from his old place anyway.
#Text=He set his laptop on the desk and wound the charger’s cord behind the wood top to an outlet.
#Text=He put his toothbrush next to the sink, but he realized he shouldn’t have worried - someone had stocked the tiny bathroom with extra toiletries.
#Text=Bucky opened the closet next to hang up the few clothes he bought, only to find that someone had stocked that, too.
#Text=He pushed through the rack: dull colours, all his size.
#Text=Bucky frowned.
#Text=Most of the shirts were t-shirts.
#Text=Fury - or one of his lackeys - hadn’t thought of everything after all.
#Text=Bucky tossed his empty bag into the closet, kicked off his shoes, and lay in his new bed.
#Text=It was surprisingly comfortable, with just the right amount of firmness.
#Text=He laced his hands behind his head and stared at the ceiling.
#Text=A few spots were discoloured.
#Text=This was probably one of the units that had been abandoned - hell, this whole
#Text=building
#Text=might’ve been abandoned, for all Bucky knew.
#Text=It would explain the slightly musty scent that lingered.
#Text=For now, this place would be a good base.
#Text=Neat and clean and functional.
#Text=From here, Bucky swore to reintegrate back into American society.
#Text=Wakanda was great for unscrambling his brain, but he’d still been on the outskirts.
#Text=By this time next year, he’d figure the new America out.
#Text=Maybe he’d even figure out Brooklyn.
#Text=Bucky spent the better part of the day researching the kid.
#Text=Fury’s files were detailed but impersonal.
#Text=If Bucky wanted to follow this kid, if he wanted to keep him safe, Bucky would need to know more than his height and eye colour.
#Text=The kid’s online presence was sparse.
#Text=Almost ridiculously non-existent, especially for a kid his age.
#Text=The privacy must have been courtesy of Tony Stark - no one else could have locked this kid down so tightly.
#Text=Facebook, Instagram, YouTube - nothing.
#Text=After a few hours of searching, Bucky did manage to dig up a digital copy of an old school newspaper.
#Text=There was a photo of the kid, and a dozen of his classmates, all clad in yellow.
#Text=Academic Decathlon
#Text=, the caption read.
#Text=Bucky smiled.
#Text=The names of all the students were spelled out under the picture, left to right.
#Text=In the photo, Parker leaned toward one boy, Ned Leeds.
#Text=Both of their faces were plastered with a goofy grin.
#Text=In Fury’s files, the Leeds boy had been listed as a ‘known associate’.
#Text=Bucky chuckled to himself.
#Text=Associate.
#Text=From the photo alone, Bucky could tell the boys were best friends.
#Text=It was a detail that couldn’t be overlooked.
#Text=He’d need to watch Leeds, too.
#Text=If anyone else ever figured out the connection between the two boys, they’d exploit it.
#Text=Bucky sighed and rolled his arm in its socket.
#Text=The new tech was good - almost unbelievably so - but no amount of medicine or advance in the prosthesis could stop the dull ache that persistently throbbed where his flesh met the metal.
#Text=Moving it was better.
#Text=When he stayed still too long, the stiffness worked its way back into Bucky’s joints.
#Text=He snapped his laptop closed.
#Text=From the closet, he fished out a grey long-sleeve t-shirt and jammed a New York Islanders baseball cap on over his hair.
#Text=As always, he buried the smooth metal of his hand in the pocket of his jeans.
#Text=The late afternoon was stifling hot.
#Text=Most people, it seemed, took refuge in the cool air-conditioned shops.

#Text=The people still on the streets had a gleam of perspiration - sweat pooled at the napes of their necks, at the place where their shirt collars met their skin, and at the underarms of their shirts.
#Text=Buck passed a small park.
#Text=From the edge of his vision, he could see a small group of kids, no more than ten, playing.
#Text=One tossed a water balloon at another girl.
#Text=She blocked it with a plastic shield - painted with red, white, and blue.
#Text=Bucky tensed and turned his head back to the street in front of him.
#Text=He kept his vision focused on the road, not straying to the sides.
#Text=When he reached the front of the kid’s apartment, he found a comfortable place on the bench across the street.
#Text=Bucky settled in, shifting on the cement, and pulled his phone out of his pocket.
#Text=He kept his head down, his eyes up, and his ears open.
#Text=He didn’t have to wait long.
#Text=The kid came up the street just over half an hour later.
#Text=He had a bag, slung over his shoulder and a carefree look on his face.
#Text=His skin tanned a shade darker than in the photo, but, if the intel on his healing factor was correct, by morning the colour would vanish.
#Text=He moved openly - he didn’t look over his shoulder.
#Text=His eyes didn’t dart from person to person.
#Text=He waved and nodded at an older woman leaving the building as he entered.
#Text=He’d be an easy target.
#Text=No wonder Fury assigned Bucky to the kid’s case - he wouldn’t last long on his own.
#Text=The kid disappeared inside for a while, but Bucky didn’t leave.
#Text=Sure enough, a few hours later, a red and blue bug crawled from a window on the fifth floor.
#Text=One of the central units, facing East, Bucky noted.
#Text=He watched the kid swing away, over the rooftops, towards the city.
#Text=Silently, Bucky followed as the kid arced above the streets of Queens.
#Text=His costume wasn’t exactly built for stealth - when there was a moment when Bucky thought he’d lost the trail, a flash of red would catch the corner of his eye.
#Text=After about an hour, the kid dropped down to the street level just before the end of Queens, right where the tunnel dips under the river into the city.
#Text=The kid surveyed the street, looking slowly from side to side.
#Text=Bucky ducked around a corner and paused too.
#Text=He cocked his head.
#Text=It wasn’t him the kid had seen.
#Text=Through the night, Bucky could hear the faint but unmistakable grind of metal against metal.
#Text=He moved from his hiding place slowly and maintained a casual gait.
#Text=The kid wasn’t visible on the main street anymore.
#Text=Bucky walked, ears tuned into the grind of metal.
#Text=He stopped at the edge of a brick warehouse, where the alley met the main street.
#Text=“Let me guess - you left your keys in your other pants?”
#Text=The kid’s voice was still as high as Bucky remembered, but there was something else now.
#Text=A bitter edge teased his tone.
#Text=The metal grinding stopped.
#Text=“Get him,” a gruff voice shouted.
#Text=Bucky heard footsteps fall against the pavement.
#Text=Three men, it sounded like, all with decent builds.
#Text=“Why is it always ‘get him’?”
#Text=The hiss of his webs thwipped, making a soft thump as one of the men fell to the ground.
#Text=“I put a lot of time and thought into my words.
#Text=I’d appreciate it if you guys would do the same.”
#Text=Another thwip of the web, another thud of a body on the pavement.
#Text=The last guy, it seems, was more nimble than the other two.
#Text=The commotion didn’t stop - the man dodged the kid’s next two webs.
#Text=The kid groaned in pain.
#Text=Bucky tensed, readying himself to join the fight.
#Text=“Low blow man,” the kid chokes out, “literally, that’s below the belt.
#Text=Not cool.”
#Text=“What are you gonna do about it?
#Text=With all your friends six feet under, who’s going to come running to help you?”
#Text=The kid didn’t reply.
#Text=Even from around the corner and even with his own head pounding with anger, Bucky felt the tension build in the air.
#Text=Through the alley, a crunch of bone cracked.
#Text=The man muffled a scream in pain and hit the ground, joining his friends.
#Text=“The police can deal with you,” the kid said before he shot up to the rooftop and disappeared back into the cover of night.
#Text=Bucky looked out.
#Text=All three of them were webbed up to the side of the building, with their tool bag at their feet.
#Text=The one in the middle was still out cold and sported a swollen eye - the faint wheeze of his breath came with a slow, grinding crunch of bone.
#Text=The kid had fractured his ribs.
#Text=Bucky swallowed and turned on his heel, back down the street.
#Text=He pulled his hat down further, over the lines of his face, least any security cameras pick him up.
#Text=He’d underestimated the kid.
#Text=So had the criminals.
#Text=But there was a rage inside the kid, one that no one had seen coming.
#Text=Bucky rubbed at the back of his neck.
#Text=This job might be more complicated than he’d thought.
#Text=Chapter 3
#Text=Over the next few weeks, Bucky fell into a comfortable routine.
#Text=He’d wake up at 7, on the dot, and exercise for an hour, always in his apartment.
#Text=He’d do push-ups, with his right arm, until his muscle rubbered, followed my squats, crunches, and the rest of the routine he’d learned in basic training.
#Text=By the end of the hour of exercise, sweat stuck his skin to his clothing - the summer had been unusually hot, and Bucky’s tiny room didn’t have AC.
#Text=After a cold shower and breakfast (two fried eggs, black coffee, and toast), he’d head down and stake out a place near the kid’s apartment.
#Text=He pushed back his weekly visit with Steve.
#Text=He watched the kid wander the city with the Leeds boy- they stopped for ice cream at colourful parkside stands.
#Text=They’d disappear inside for chunks of time, playing video games and watching seasons of TV shows.
#Text=The kid was still on summer break, so his schedule was chaotic - the only consistent part Bucky could count on was the fact the kid wouldn’t stir until at least ten in the morning.
#Text=Even later, some days.
#Text=Between the typical teenage laziness and the late nights the kid pulled while patrolling, Bucky was surprised he got up at all some days.
#Text=Sam’s mission got extended.
#Text=Bucky watched the kid.
#Text=He’d swing through the city with ease - he usually kept to Queens and Manhattan, but occasionally the kid dipped into Brooklyn if he caught a hint of any noise.
#Text=After the first night, the kid hadn’t hurt anyone - not seriously, at least.
#Text=Kid was always pulling his punches.
#Text=The city was quiet, more so than Bucky could ever remember it, but he still watched the kid.
#Text=Petty theft was on the rise, and that was precisely the sort of thing the kid went after.
#Text=Bucky watched the kid, always tense.
#Text=Always ready to intervene if needed.
#Text=The kid didn’t need him.
#Text=He held his own in every fight, even though he held back nearly all his strength.
#Text=That sort of control...
#Text=Bucky struggled for months before he could hold a piece of fruit without his metal fingers puncturing the skin.
#Text=He remembered - despite the scrambled mess of his brain - trying to hold a peach, once.
#Text=A rare moment of lucidity.
#Text=It was the 80s if the music was anything to go by.
#Text=Somewhere warm, maybe Monaco.
#Text=Maybe Cannes.
#Text=He reached for the fruit piled neatly on the stand.
#Text=The peach, under his hand, exploded in a mess of pulp.
#Text=The juice ran down his hand and pooled on the cobblestone.
#Text=He couldn’t remember what happened next, no matter how much he raked through his thoughts.
#Text=It was just one of the gaps he’d have to learn to live with.
#Text=The kid’s control was precise.
#Text=He didn’t have nightmares about being wrought into a weapon by Hydra.
#Text=He probably didn’t think about what would happen if he
#Text=lost
#Text=that control of his - even when the kid was angry, he only cracked a rib.
#Text=The kid didn’t have to worry that there might be forgotten bugs or hidden codewords still tucked away inside his head.
#Text=Maybe that was why Fury assigned Bucky to the case - he worried about Hydra and super soldiers and brainwashing enough for the both of them.
#Text=One night, the kid stayed late at the Leeds boy’s house.
#Text=They watched cartoons (if the spectre of television light against the window was anything to go off of) until three in the morning.
#Text=Bucky observed from his battered Honda Civic (courtesy of Fury) as the kid slipped out the window and flung himself into the night.
#Text=Once the kid shimmied into his place, it was nearly four in the morning.
#Text=Bucky headed back to his apartment.
#Text=He didn’t leave the parking garage.
#Text=Instead, he backed the civic into the stall, pulled the familiar set of keys from the inner pocket of his jacket, and revved the engine of his bike.
#Text=It hummed to life with a comforting purr.
#Text=The world and roads were dark, but Bucky knew the route.
#Text=He kept the handles steady, despite the bumps in the road, until the sun slipped over the horizon.
#Text=The thoughts came: flashes of unfamiliar cities; shouts of foreign languages; blows to his body that Bucky couldn’t feel.
#Text=At the time, those moments of clarity were gifts.
#Text=Now, he pushed them down and set his jaw and focused on the passing world of upstate New York.
#Text=He reached Shady Oaks just after nine in the morning.
#Text=The nurse wasn’t Leanna - she’d older and more stern.
#Text=She didn’t welcome Bucky; she only pointed at the log book.
#Text=“Sign here.”
#Text=He did.
#Text=He knew the routine.
#Text=He scribbled his - or James Carter’s- signature into the square, inked in ‘9:08 a.m.’ in the next box, and set off down the hallway towards the stairwell.
#Text=“Excuse me,” the nurse said.
#Text=Bucky turned and raised his eyebrows.
#Text=“You have to use the hand sanitizer before entering.”
#Text=She jerked her chin toward a laminated piece of paper next to a plastic dispenser.
#Text=“We can’t risk spreading germs.”
#Text=“Of course we can’t,” Bucky grumbled.
#Text=He walked back toward the stand, turned his back to the nurse, and pumped the liquid into his hand.
#Text=The nurse had a sharp eye - the last thing he needed was her catching a flash of the dark metal and connecting some dots that would be better left alone.
#Text=“It’s the home’s policy.
#Text=You better get in the habit of doing it every time.”
#Text=“I will,” Bucky said with false sweetness as he walked away, his left hand shoved back into his pocket.
#Text=When he reached Steve’s room, he was reading the morning paper and sipping at a mug of dark coffee.
#Text=“Buck,” he said with a smile.
#Text=“I wasn’t expecting you.”
#Text=Bucky pulled him into a hug.
#Text=“I’ve been busy, but I had some PTO.
#Text=Well, I made some PTO.”
#Text=Steve chuckled.
#Text=“Not going AWOL, are you?”
#Text=“Maybe a little.”
#Text=Bucky helped himself to a mug and coffee from the pot on the counter of Steve’s small kitchenette.
#Text=“Some vacation time isn’t a bad thing.
#Text=God, Buck.
#Text=Peggy and I, we had this - this cottage,” he started, his voice choked.
#Text=Even in all of Bucky’s visits, Steve had never spoken about his life - his other life.
#Text=“We had this cottage out in Montauk.
#Text=It was a shitty old thing - no matter how much I tried to fix it, the wind still rattled the window panes and blew sand inside.
#Text=But when you were out there, there wasn’t anything in the world you could worry about if you tried.
#Text=There was only the dunes of the beach and the ocean stretching out as far as you could see.”
#Text=Steve’s lip quivered.
#Text=“You should find yourself a Montauk, Buck.”
#Text=Bucky bit his tongue.
#Text=“I’ll try.”
#Text=He knew his voice was flat and dead, but he didn’t bother trying to hide it.
#Text=Steve cleared his throat and wiped at his eyes.
#Text=“So, who’s got you roped into this job?”
#Text=Steve’s voice sounded casual, but Bucky had known him long enough to hear the lilt of suspension buried in his words.
#Text=“It’s Fury - don’t worry.”
#Text=Buck sat in the armchair next to the open window.
#Text=“I’m watching the kid’s back.”
#Text=“Queens?”
#Text=Bucky nodded.
#Text=“Fury thinks there’s a chance someone might come for him - especially now that Stark’s gone and you’re ... well, you’re not exactly on the board either.”
#Text=Steve frowned and cast his gaze at the floorboards.
#Text=“Is the threat any credible?”
#Text=“I haven’t seen anything yet.
#Text=All the channels I’ve tried to monitor are dead  - even Hydra is still recovering from the snap.”
#Text=“But it could be?”
#Text=Bucky sighed.
#Text=“The kid... what he can do, Steve, it’s incredible.
#Text=He caught Stark’s attention for a good reason.”
#Text=“Other people are bound to notice.”
#Text=Bucky nodded in affirmation.
#Text=“He’s lucky someone else didn’t find him before Stark...”
#Text=Bucky trailed off, unsure what else to say.
#Text=Steve, thankfully, pivoted the topic.
#Text=“The world’s still in recovery, I think.
#Text=Everyone is shocked and holding on to what they have for dear life.”
#Text=“But,” Bucky said.
#Text=Steve smiled wistfully.
#Text=“But it’s not going to last.
#Text=This summer’s been a blessing.
#Text=The cracks are going to start to show.
#Text=There’s going to be shortages of food and water.
#Text=Unemployment is the highest it’s ever been.
#Text=All of the horrible things we grew up with in the 30s - it’s all going to come back with a vengeance.
#Text=Over those five years, the world adjusted to living with only half the population.
#Text=It’s going to take some time to readjust.”
#Text=Bucky nodded slowly.
#Text=He rubbed at his temple and tried to ignore the ache of his head.
#Text=“Hey - at least there’s more varieties of Coca - Cola than there was in the 30s.
#Text=I swear there must’ve been a dozen kinds when I was at the grocery store the other day.”
#Text=“Don’t get me started - when I first settled into 2012 I thought half of the flavours of potato chips were a joke,” he said with a laugh.
#Text=Bucky wrinkled his nose.
#Text=“Food doesn’t taste the same, though.
#Text=It’s too... plastic.”
#Text=Steve nodded, once, in agreement.
#Text=“It tastes like shit.”

#Text=For a moment, as they laughed, the ghost of the years between them slipped into the ether - they were just two kids from Brooklyn again, tumbling around the city with hearts too big for their heads.
#Text=Bucky got back to Queens late in the afternoon.
#Text=Sleep prickled at his eyes, but over the years he’d become used to fighting that sensation.
#Text=Before he went back to his apartment, he drove past the kid’s place to check and make sure everything was still as quiet as when he’d left.
#Text=Bucky didn’t have to look hard - the kid was walking out of his building, straightening out a collared shirt, with a bouquet of wrapped flowers in his hand.
#Text=Even from a distance, the nervous energy the kid emitted was palpable.
#Text=Kid had a date.
#Text=Bucky smirked.
#Text=He pulled his bike into a lot around the corner and followed the kid, from a comfortable distance, as he walked off into the city.
#Text=In the subway, Bucky got on the car behind the one the kid hopped into and settled between a lost tourist and two gossiping tweens in a spot with a clean line of sight to the doors.
#Text=In midtown, the kid got off.
#Text=Bucky followed.
#Text=It was easier to hide in the thick of the city than it was in the suburbs - the rush of people provided an excellent cover.
#Text=It was so simple to slip behind someone taller, or turn to a side street, or dip into a shop.
#Text=Outside of a Korean restaurant, the kid stopped.
#Text=He fiddled with his collar again and tried to make the flip of the fabric sit flat.
#Text=Buck slipped into the coffee shop across the street.
#Text=He ordered the largest and sweetest-sounding drink on the menu and sat at a round table next to the window.
#Text=From his spot, he could see the entrance of the restaurant and the kid (practically bouncing) waiting out in front.
#Text=After a few minutes, a tall girl with a twist of curly dark hair walked up to the kid.
#Text=She smiled, and he hugged her, awkwardly, before handing her the flowers as though they were an afterthought.
#Text=They went inside.
#Text=Bucky stayed in his place, sipping at the disgustingly sweet (Bucky didn’t even know it
#Text=could
#Text=be that sweet) coffee that glazed his mouth.
#Text=Somehow, even ordering the drink he wanted felt too personal.
#Text=Bucky kept his eyes fixed on the restaurant and took in the scene of the street.
#Text=A steady stream of people swam by, all absorbed in their own lives.
#Text=A young woman pecked a man’s cheek.
#Text=A family walked along, the kid in the middle holding his parents’ hands and swing her feet in the air.
#Text=A pack of friends laughed with each other, absorbed in conversation.
#Text=Bucky swirled his cup around and set it on the table.
#Text=He shifted his metal arm in its socket.
#Text=Stay focused on the mission,
#Text=he reminded himself.
#Text=After nearly two hours, the kid and his date walked out.
#Text=The sun was glowing as it set, and the tension had visibly faded from the kid’s face.
#Text=They set off uptown, leaning in close together but not quite touching.
#Text=Bucky tossed his half-empty cup in the crash and traced their path.
#Text=He wove through the crowd, keeping his eyes on the kid’s crop of sandy-brown hair and the girl’s messy curls.
#Text=Somewhere around 36th and Madison, he lost them at the turn of a crossing light.
#Text=Bucky frowned.
#Text=The kid’s guard was down right now - he’d be vulnerable to any attack.
#Text=Bucky studied the shops along the street, looking for any place they might’ve ducked into.
#Text=A glint of a dark twist of hair - the girl’s - caught the edge of Bucky’s sight as they turned a corner.
#Text=Bucky pulled the top of his hat lower and turned on his heel, following the kid and his date.
#Text=As Bucky turned the corner, a hand clipped his shoulder - his good one.
#Text=Long nails pricked into his skin and wrenched him back.
#Text=“What do you think you’re doing?”
#Text=Bucky blinked.
#Text=The girl -the kid’s date - scowled back at him.
#Text=Her face twisted in malcontent.
#Text=Chapter 4
#Text=The girl narrowed her gaze.
#Text=Behind her, the kid stood, frowning, tensed, and ready to spring.
#Text=When his eye’s met Bucky’s, the concern fell off his face.
#Text=“Oh.
#Text=It’s you.”
#Text=The kid stepped forward, to the girl’s side, and put his hand on hers.
#Text=“It’s okay, M.J.”
#Text=She raised her eyebrow at him.
#Text=“I told you we were being followed.”
#Text=She let go of Bucky’s shoulder.
#Text=“He’s a - a friend.
#Text=I guess.”
#Text=The kid scratched his head.
#Text=“Why
#Text=are
#Text=you following us?”
#Text=Bucky glanced between the two of them - the girl crossed her arms over her chest (wrapped flowers still in hand) and frowned, clearly not ready to believe anything Bucky would say.
#Text=The kid wore a familiar look of confusion, but his arms hung lazily by his side.
#Text=“A mutual friend asked me to keep an eye on you,” Bucky said.
#Text=“A mutual friend?”
#Text=The kid’s face screwed up in thought.
#Text=“Do you mean Fury?”
#Text=For fuck’s sake.
#Text=“You really don’t get the whole ‘covert’ thing, do you?”
#Text=“It’s alright.
#Text=M.J. knows about my, uh, my bug... problem.”
#Text=“I figured it out,” the girl said.
#Text=She stayed still and suspicious, but her gaze twitched toward the kid.
#Text=She’d pieced it together - if she could figure it out, then so could someone else if they watched close enough.
#Text=“We are in
#Text=public
#Text=.
#Text=First rule - never assume you’re not being watched.
#Text=Bugs can be anywhere, especially these days.
#Text=We can talk about the details later.”
#Text=“We - we don’t need to talk about this later,” the kid said, trying to sound intimidating.
#Text=“I can look out for myself.”
#Text=“Sure you can,” Bucky said.
#Text=“I think you two should talk,” the girl said.
#Text=“My dad’s place is only a block from here, I can walk myself home.”
#Text=The kid’s face faltered.
#Text=“Text me when you’re home?”
#Text=She nodded, and the two hugged, less awkward than when they first met.
#Text=Bucky watched the kid watching her as she took off uptown.
#Text=“What’s the real reason you’re here?”
#Text=Bucky shifted in his spot.
#Text=“I told you.
#Text=I’ve been asked to watch your back.”
#Text=The kid eyed Bucky.
#Text=“You don’t have to believe me, but it’s true.”
#Text=Bucky moved toward the nearest entrance to the subway, and the kid followed at his heels.
#Text=“I can watch my own back.”
#Text=“No shame in having someone watch your six.”
#Text=Bucky bought tickets for both of them - he handed the kid the white and green paper.
#Text=The kid said nothing.
#Text=He stared at Bucky, his puppy-dog eyes drifting from his faded baseball cap to his work boots as if he were trying to make a mental checklist of every one of Bucky’s features.
#Text=When the train came, they got on, the kid still following Bucky - had it not occurred to him that if he really didn’t want to be here, he could just walk away?
#Text=Bucky took a seat in the subway car and the kid sat directly across the aisle.
#Text=He folded his arms and puffed up his chest.
#Text=Bucky turned his laugh into a cough.
#Text=“Look,” he said.
#Text=“I don’t care if you want me to watch out for you or not - I’m going to be doing it with or without your permission.”
#Text=The kid rolled his eyes.
#Text=He
#Text=was
#Text=still a teenager, Bucky reminded himself, even if he could lift a bus.
#Text=“Of course, it would be much easier for the both of us if you accepted a little help.”
#Text=Bucky shrugged.
#Text=“But what do
#Text=I know
#Text=.”
#Text=“Don’t you have anything better to do?”
#Text=The kid looked at Bucky pointedly.
#Text=Not really.
#Text=“I told our friend I’d look out for you.
#Text=I have no intention of letting him down.”
#Text=“I’m not stepping aside, either.”
#Text=The kid’s voice rose with a note of challenge.
#Text=“I don’t want to hear how I’m ‘not ready’ or ‘too young’.
#Text=You’re not gonna sideline me.”
#Text=“You’re what kid, sixteen?”
#Text=“Seventeen.”
#Text=Bucky leaned forward, not wanting anyone to overhear.
#Text=“Look, kid - I’m the last person who’s gonna sideline you for being too young.
#Text=In - in the war,” Bucky started.
#Text=He hadn’t expected his voice to still catch when he spoke about, well, the start of everything.
#Text=“There were lots of soldiers who could barely grow patchy stubble.
#Text=They were your age, or even younger, but they lied on their enlistment forms.
#Text=Couldn’t stand to sit back and watch while others fought.”
#Text=The kid opened his mouth to say something, but Bucky cut him off.
#Text=“I’m not encouraging it.
#Text=But I do understand.”
#Text=They rode the rest of the way to Queens in silence.
#Text=The kid kept eyeing him, but Bucky didn’t mind.
#Text=He studied the rest of the subway car, taking in the array of people and mentally cataloging possible escape routes and noting any threats.
#Text=He wished he could turn it off sometimes, but he could never snap off the overactivity in his head.
#Text=In some ways, it was alright.
#Text=He never went without a plan.
#Text=Mostly, he just wanted to rest and relax his muscles and to see a place for its beauty instead of an array of potential threats strung together.
#Text=When the train rolled into the station nearest to the kid’s Bucky stood before the kid did.
#Text=The kid’s face - which had softened slightly toward the end of the ride - hardened into a tight mask again.
#Text=“It’s creepy that you know where I live.”
#Text=Bucky let the kid lead the way to his apartment.
#Text=“I’ve been called worse, kid.”
#Text=They reached the apartment building and Bucky sighed, resigned.
#Text=He’d expected the kid would’ve cracked by now.
#Text=“You’re not coming inside.”
#Text=The kid paused before the glass door.
#Text=“And I still don’t need your help.”
#Text=Bucky chucked.
#Text=“Let me give you my number and address.”
#Text=The kid started to protest, but Bucky raised his hand in the air in mock surrender.
#Text=“You can call me to tell me how much you don’t need my help.
#Text=Hell - you can knock on my door at three in the morning to show me how fine you’re doing by yourself.
#Text=Alright?”
#Text=The kid hesitated.
#Text=“Fine.”
#Text=He shoved his hand into his pocket and handed Bucky a sleek new phone - one of Stark’s.
#Text=The comment started forming on Bucky’s tongue - a casual remark that this model wasn’t due out until next month - but he held it back.
#Text=He wasn’t ready to touch any topic related to Stark just yet, no matter how tangential.
#Text=Bucky held the phone his left hand and typed his phone number and address in with his right index finger.
#Text=The kid snorts.
#Text=“What?”
#Text=“You text like someone twice your age.”
#Text=Bucky grimaced as he handed back the phone.
#Text=“I didn’t think you’d appreciate me cracking the screen.”
#Text=He flexes his metal hand - his control is better than he’d ever thought possible, but he still doesn’t trust it, especially with delicate things.
#Text=“Oh.”
#Text=The kid scratched the back of his head and looked at Bucky, his face open.
#Text=“And uh, I’m sorry, Mr.
#Text=Barnes.”
#Text=“About what?”
#Text=“The uh - airport thing.
#Text=In Berlin.”
#Text=Bucky chuckled.
#Text=“I’m sorry for interrupting your date.
#Text=Word of advice - if you’re bringing her flowers, give them to her at her place before the date.
#Text=That way she can put them in water.
#Text=Stops them from wilting.”
#Text=The kid nodded and Bucky left him at the apartment doors.
#Text=He found his bike in the lot across the street and speed back to his unit, his eye barely registering the road or traffic.
#Text=When he reached his door, he kicked off his boots and shed his sweater.
#Text=He flopped in the bed, with his jeans still on, and welcomed the sleep that washed over him.
#Text=When he was tired like this - exhausted from his brain to his bones - he didn’t dream.
#Text=Bucky wished for more nights like this.
#Text=A few days later, in the last week of August, Sam rolled back into town.
#Text=It was nearly three weeks later than when he’d initially planned to return and he was sporting a shiny black eye and split lip.
#Text=“What can I say?
#Text=It comes with the job,” he said with a laugh.
#Text=He finished off the end of his beer and raised his hand to the bartender to order another.
#Text=“The ladies dig it.
#Text=They all coo over how injured I am - how
#Text=noble
#Text=I am.”
#Text=Bucky swirled his beer around in the glass.
#Text=He couldn’t get drunk, and he didn’t care too much for the taste, but he’d come out with Sam for the socialization.
#Text=“There’s some great nurses up at Steve’s place if you’re looking for someone.
#Text=Hell, they probably have an extra bed - I can call and check you in next week.”
#Text=Bucky smirked into his drink.
#Text=Sam shook his head and launched into the vague details of the mission.
#Text=The bar was seedy enough that no one would expect them to be there, but they always watched their words.
#Text=It was nice
#Text=, Bucky thought vaguely,
#Text=to have someone to talk to.
#Text=For as long as Bucky could remember, all his social interactions were defined by some obligation.

#Text=There was always Steve, of course, but in the army, his relationship with the other Commandos was born out of necessity and chance - they were captured together, and stepped up to fight together.
#Text=In all of Bucky’s lost years, he remembered only pieces of conversations and fragments of something that resembled companionship.
#Text=In his lost years, he wasn’t human - he was a machine with a metal arm and a faulty wired brain.
#Text=The only people who spoke to him were either ones authorized to handle the asset or marks.
#Text=Bucky took a swig of beer and pushed the thoughts down.
#Text=Then there was Steve.
#Text=Again.
#Text=In Wakanda, he was a project.
#Text=Someone who drew in prosthetic designers and experts in psychology and politicians.
#Text=It wasn’t that he wasn’t thankful - he owed them his life - but they weren’t people who voluntarily came into Bucky’s life.
#Text=They weren’t people who stuck by his side once the obligation was gone.
#Text=Sam did stick around, but Bucky wasn’t exactly sure he’d call the guy a friend.
#Text=He wouldn’t call him up at any hour of the night, they wouldn’t be best men at each other’s weddings or any of that sentimental shit.
#Text=Drinking buddies,
#Text=Bucky decided on.
#Text=That was the most accurate way to describe their relationship.
#Text=“So,” Sam said, picking up his second pint (they always need the first one to get through his stories).
#Text=“What’s been keeping you busy?”
#Text=“Well, I moved.”
#Text=“Didn’t like the place in Brooklyn?”
#Text=“Something like that.”
#Text=Bucky sipped at his drink.
#Text=He only ever ordered one - anything more would be a waste of money.
#Text=“But, I uh, actually got a job.
#Text=Or an assignment, at least.”
#Text=“Oh, did you now?”
#Text=Same raised his eyebrows.
#Text=“Do I even want to know”
#Text=Bucky smirks into his drink.
#Text=“I’m looking after the bug boy.”
#Text=Sam choked on his lager.
#Text=“The one who tried to arrest me?”
#Text=“What, do you have multiple bug-themed children trying to arrest you?”
#Text=Sam sank back in his seat.
#Text=“How much is Fury paying you?”
#Text=“Enough.
#Text=Got a decent place out in Queens covered too.”
#Text=Bucky rubbed at his stubble, not sure how much to say.
#Text=“It’s... complicated.
#Text=There might be people looking for him.”
#Text=Sam nodded, understanding.
#Text=“Well, if one of those, uh,
#Text=interested parties
#Text=gets to be too much, just let me know.”
#Text=“I think the power of that red and blue frisbee went to your head.
#Text=I’ll manage my job - you can worry about yours.”
#Text=Sam grew thoughtful.
#Text=“Alright.
#Text=Offer always stands though, you know that.”
#Text=“Of course.”
#Text=Sam nodded, again, shifted in his spot and paused.
#Text=“Did I ever tell you about the time I spent labour day I spent in Montreal with my ex?
#Text=I mean, she wasn’t my ex at the time...”
#Text=He was a natural storyteller, Bucky would admit that, and by the time his beer became froth at the bottom of an empty glass, the hollowness inside his chest closes up a bit.
#Text=As Sam cleared up with the bartender, the mechanic chime of Bucky's phone rang through the dingy dive.
#Text=It took him a second before he realized that it even
#Text=was
#Text=his phone - he had hardly heard it sound.
#Text=Bucky ducked out the door, into the alley.
#Text=“Hello?”
#Text=“Hello?
#Text=Mr.
#Text=Barnes?”
#Text=The voice on the other end squeaked with nerve.
#Text=“Kid?”
#Text=“I, uh, talked with M.J. last night.”
#Text=“Did you make a decision?”
#Text=“I, uh, well I’ve got some rules - some terms of agreement.
#Text=But I guess it’s alright if you wanna stalk me.”
#Text=Bucky’s lips twitched into a smile.
#Text=“Smart choice.”
#Text=Mentally, he added the kid to the list of people who were only in his life because of duty.
#Text=The list made it easier to stay unattached - Bucky wouldn’t mistake obligation for care.
#Text=He couldn’t afford to be so careless.
#Text=Chapter 5
#Text=Chapter Notes
#Text=Did y'all see that Far From Home trailer?
#Text=It was just too much.
#Text=The sizzle of eggs on a frying pan and the acrid warmth of coffee filled Bucky’s small apartment.
#Text=Bucky tucked the kid’s file into the drawer of his desk, closed his laptop, and straightened the sheets and pillows on his bed.
#Text=At ten, on the dot, someone buzzed up.
#Text=Kid must’ve been waiting outside.
#Text=Even still, Bucky answered - he couldn’t risk taking the chance.
#Text=“Hello?”
#Text=“It’s me,” the kid’s voice came through.
#Text=Bucky let him in and waited by his unit’s door.
#Text=He didn’t need the prying eyes of neighbours and Bucky was sure that Mrs.
#Text=Martinez in the corner apartment would see the kid as an open invitation to interrogate Bucky about his life.
#Text=He opened the door before the kid even knocked.
#Text=The kid stood in the entrance way, his backpack slung over one shoulder and wearing a plain white t-shirt with the image of a bubbling beaker splayed across the center.
#Text=Underneath, the caption read ‘you’re overreacting’.
#Text=Bucky snorted to himself.
#Text=Wide-eyed, the kid took in Bucky’s place: empty bookshelf; plain white walls; grey tile floor.
#Text=“This is - this is a nice place.”
#Text=“You’re a horrible liar, kid, you know that?”
#Text=Bucky flipped his breakfast onto a plate and sat at the small metal table in the kitchen.
#Text=The kid joined on the other side, looking wistfully at Bucky’s plate of food.
#Text=“You hungry?”
#Text=The kid continued to stare at Bucky’s food.
#Text=“No.”
#Text=Faintly, Bucky heard the kid’s stomach rumble.
#Text=Bucky sighed.
#Text=“What did I
#Text=just
#Text=tell you?”
#Text=He stood and moved back to the fridge, pulling out the carton of eggs and cracked two back into the frying pan.
#Text=“I did eat this morning,” the kid protested, “it’s just that I’m hungry all the time.”
#Text=“So was Steve.”
#Text=The guy was a pit - Bucky vaguely wondered how much of his salary he spent on food.
#Text=Whatever Bucky was injected with, it hadn’t affected him to the same extent.
#Text=Sure, he ate more than most, but it wasn’t something he actively had to worry about.
#Text=The kid looked like he wanted to say something more, but chose to keep quiet and watch Bucky cook.
#Text=“Thanks,” he said when Bucky set the plate of toast and eggs in front of him.
#Text=“Don’t let yourself get too hungry.
#Text=It could make you vulnerable to an attack.”
#Text=The kid swallowed his mouthful of scrambled egg.
#Text=“Thanks for that advice.”
#Text=Bucky dug into his plate of food.
#Text=“You’re welcome.”
#Text=“So,” the kid started, “if you’re really serious about this - this whole ‘stalking’ thing -”
#Text=“Watching out for you, but go ahead.”
#Text=“I have rules.”
#Text=“Shoot.”
#Text=Bucky topped up his mug of coffee and gestured at the kid.
#Text=He shook his head.
#Text=“First rule - you can’t interrupt me when I’m with my friends.
#Text=And especially not when I’m on a date.”
#Text=“Fair enough,” Bucky said, “but don’t forget that technically
#Text=she
#Text=found
#Text=me.
#Text=”
#Text=The kid ignored that comment and another forkful of egg and toast into his mouth.
#Text=“Second rule - no telling me to step down.”
#Text=“I already told you that I wouldn’t do that.”
#Text=“Third - no interacting with anyone else I know.
#Text=If they see you, the story is that you’re my Uncle.
#Text=A relative from my mom’s side that crawled out of the woodwork.
#Text=But you can’t seek them out.”
#Text=“Alright,” Bucky said.
#Text=He plucked an apple out of the bowl on the table.
#Text=“Does your Aunt know?”
#Text=“I haven’t told her yet, but I will tonight.”
#Text=“Okay.”
#Text=Bucks sighed.
#Text=“But I have rules too.”
#Text=“That’s not what -”
#Text=“First rule - we stick to the cover story.
#Text=If it’s going to work, you’ve got to invest in it too.”
#Text=The kid frowned.
#Text=“I guess so.”
#Text=“Second - I get access to your location.
#Text=If I’m not going to be around you all the time, I need another reliable way to help if you get in trouble.”
#Text=Under the kid’s breath, he grumbled something about babysitting.
#Text=Bucky chose to ignore that.
#Text=“Third - you gotta train with me.”
#Text=“Train, like,” the kid mimed punching.
#Text=“Yep,” Bucky said.
#Text=“You need to learn to defend yourself if I’m not there.”
#Text=“I’ve gotten by so far.”
#Text=“That’s true, but none of the people you’ve fought have been able to go toe to toe with you.”
#Text=“Hey - that’s not fair.
#Text=In Berlin -”
#Text=“In Berlin, we were all holding back.
#Text=No one was fighting with the intent to seriously harm, let alone kill.
#Text=You need to learn to fight and not just rely on your strength alone.”
#Text=“Fine,” the kid spat.
#Text=“Is that everything?”
#Text=“I think so.”
#Text=The kid reached into his backpack and pulled out a ballpoint pen and a red coiled notebook.
#Text=From the back, he tore loose a page.
#Text=Across the top of the jagged-edged paper, he wrote: Rules for Stalking Peter.
#Text=Underneath the large heading, he scrawled down his three stipulations.
#Text=“Do I get to add mine?”
#Text=“I guess,” he said and pushed the paper toward Bucky.
#Text=Under Peter’s rules, he wrote: Bucky’s Rules for The Kid.
#Text=In his neat, loopy handwriting, he added his three conditions.
#Text=“That look alright?”
#Text=“I guess.”
#Text=The kid took the paper back from Bucky and walked up to his fridge.
#Text=“Do you have a magnet?”
#Text=Bucky shook his head.
#Text=“Tape?”
#Text=“To your right - in the top drawer.”
#Text=The kid ripped off a piece and stuck their rule list in the center of Bucky’s fridge.
#Text=“We’re gonna stick to this, okay?”
#Text=“Sure kid, of course, we will.”
#Text=That night, as the kid headed out on his patrol, Bucky didn’t follow him.
#Text=Instead, he opened his laptop and watched the red flicker of the tracker weave through the streets of New York.
#Text=The next day, at six in the morning, the kid showed up at the edge of Forest Park, as they’d agreed.
#Text=His eyes were rimmed with red and his tufts of hair stuck out in wild directions.
#Text=“How’d you get up this early?”
#Text=Bucky stretched his arms.
#Text=The cool morning meant that his long-sleeve shirt wouldn’t draw the same attention as it would mid-day.
#Text=Besides, the cool weather was better for jogging.
#Text=“You agreed to the training.
#Text=This is part of it.”
#Text=“I thought you meant, like, cool ninja-assassin moves.
#Text=Not early morning runs.”
#Text=“This is just as much a part of learning the ropes as the other techniques.”
#Text=The kid’s eyes perked up.
#Text=“So we
#Text=will
#Text=get to the ninja-assassin ass-kicking?”
#Text=“Sure, kid.
#Text=Eventually.”
#Text=“I don’t believe you.”
#Text=They took off at an easy pace.
#Text=Bucky lead the way, jogging along, while the kid followed easily behind him.
#Text=What he lacked in form, he made up for in natural atheism.
#Text=He swung his arms too widely and he didn’t bring his knees up high enough.
#Text=“Is that as fast as you can go?”
#Text=Bucky rolled his eyes.
#Text=“It’s not about speed, kid.
#Text=They loop through the trails of the park - gravel and flanked by a spread of trees and underbrush.
#Text=As Bucky’s feet hit the ground, he imagined this small slice of forest spreading out over New York.
#Text=Instead of skyscrapers, there would be a tangle of aspen and birch.
#Text=There would be deer instead of trucks.
#Text=Fish in the Hudson instead of trash.
#Text=It might be nice.
#Text=Bucky shook his head clear and fell back into the rhythm of his pace.
#Text=Someone else had thought like that - there was no point in lamenting about what was lost.
#Text=They needed to focus on forging a better future.
#Text=He pulled away from the greenery of the park, back into the thick of Queens.
#Text=Their feet steadily struck the cement of the sidewalks.
#Text=Cars and bikers and other joggers filtered past, but the streets were still quiet when they reached the place they had started once again.
#Text=After the first three loops, the kid’s pace faltered while Bucky’s remained as steady as when he’d started.
#Text=Around the fifth mile of their loop through the park and surrounding neighbourhood, the kid strained to keep up with Bucky.
#Text=His breathing sounded shallow, uneven and gasping.
#Text=“Everything alright?”
#Text=“Yeah, it’s - I’m good.”
#Text=He wheezed.
#Text=“Great.”
#Text=Bucky slowed to a stop.
#Text=“Kid?”
#Text=A drop of sweat trickled down and pooled at his lower back, but he wasn’t anywhere close to being out of breath.
#Text=The kid pressed his hands against his knees.
#Text=“I’ll be alright.
#Text=Just - give me a minute.”
#Text=Bucky hesitated, unsure of what to do.
#Text=“Does this normally happen?”
#Text=The kid shook his head.
#Text=“Well, I don’t normally jog.”
#Text=He stretched again and took a deep breath, finally, to Bucky’s pleasure.
#Text=“I think it’s, uh, a mental thing.”
#Text=“Oh?”
#Text=The kid nodded.
#Text=“I - before all this, I used to have asthma.”
#Text=“Of course you did.”
#Text=Bucky grimaced.
#Text=The world flickered, and he was fifteen again, standing next to Steve in an alley or behind the school.
#Text=With asthma came fear.
#Text=If there was another guy giving Steve a tough time, Bucky could punch him away.
#Text=He couldn’t stop the problem that brewed inside.
#Text=“Sometimes, when I think about how hard all this used to be, I just panic.
#Text=It’s like my breath gets caught inside my chest,” the kid said, rubbing at his chest and damp shirt, but thankfully breathing steadily.
#Text=“I - I’m sorry for ending the training.”
#Text=“It’s alright, kid.
#Text=I should have started smaller.”
#Text=As they walked back toward the kid’s place in Queens, the sun climbed higher in the sky.
#Text=More people exited their buildings, and a steady stream of cars flowed on the streets.
#Text=“You gotta raise your knees higher.
#Text=When your feet come down, right now they’re stopping your momentum when they fall.
#Text=Land in the middle of your feet.”
#Text=The kid’s face twisted in thought.
#Text=“I didn’t realize there was a proper way to run.
#Text=I thought it was just natural.”
#Text=“I told you - technique is important.
#Text=If you wanna be at the top of your game, you gotta train for it.”
#Text=The kid noded, once.
#Text=When they reached his place, Bucky left him standing there.
#Text=“Take a shower, have some food and get some rest,” he instructed.
#Text=“We’ll do this again tomorrow.”
#Text=“But it’s my first day of school.”
#Text=“Sorry - next time aliens are destroying the earth, I’ll just tell them to come back when you don’t have school.”
#Text=The kid gave him a pointed look.
#Text=“I’ll see you tomorrow, Mr.
#Text=Barnes.”
#Text=“See you, kid.”
#Text=The next day, Monday, they do the same - meet at the edge of the park at six.
#Text=They jog, again, but slower this time.
#Text=Bucky stopped every quarter mile or so and let the kid rest.
#Text=They practiced short sprints over the path and Bucky corrected the kid’s wonky gait.
#Text=Just before seven, Bucky called it a day.
#Text=The kid looked better - he resembled an actual jogger instead of a baby deer.
#Text=Bucky swung the keys to his civic around his hand as they walked back to the kid’s place.
#Text=“You ready to go to school.”
#Text=“I can just take the subway.”
#Text=“That wasn’t an option.”
#Text=The kid shrugged - he knew it was a losing fight.
#Text=“Fine.
#Text=I just need to shower and change.
#Text=I’ll be ten, maybe fifteen minutes.”
#Text=“Fine,” Bucky said as he leaned against the wall outside.
#Text=The kid eyed him.
#Text=“You don’t have to wait out here.
#Text=You can come up.”
#Text=It
#Text=would
#Text=be advantageous to case the kid’s apartment.
#Text=He followed the kid up the stairs without protest.
#Text=As they climbed, the kid greeted his neighbours with a warm smile.
#Text=They entered his place and the kid showed Bucky into the living room.
#Text=“Have a seat,” he said.
#Text=“I’ll be quick.”
#Text=Bucky nodded.
#Text=The kid slammed the bathroom door, and Bucky stood, taking the room in.
#Text=The place was nice, as far as apartments went.
#Text=There was a window on the East side, out of which he could make out the faint city skyline.
#Text=Photographs dotted the walls.
#Text=Some were of the kid, but there were a fair number of other pictures of people who’d filled the kid’s file.
#Text=In the little headshots clipped to the document, it was easier to think of them as just a name.
#Text=It was harder to remain detached when he stared into their smiling eyes at Conney Island or when he studied the way their hands folded together at a wedding.
#Text=All of these people, happy and living, blissfully unaware of how the world would twist in a few short years.
#Text=Bucky wrenched his head away, forcing himself to change his line of thought.
#Text=The decorations were carefully chosen.
#Text=On the far wall, a bookcase was packed full.
#Text=Trinkets, potted plants, and small sculptures were carefully arranged between the rows of books - many on colour theory, design, and artists.
#Text=“Those are mine if you’re wondering.”
#Text=Bucky almost started at the noise but measured his reaction.
#Text=He turned to see the kid’s aunt, May, standing in the front of him, her long hair twisted over her shoulder.
#Text=“They’re nice,” he said, trying to sound casual.
#Text=Truthfully, he hadn’t expected her to be home.
#Text=He figured the kid would’ve mentioned.
#Text=“I’m May,” she said.
#Text=“James,” Bucky said and shook her hand.
#Text=“It’s nice to meet you, ma’am.”
#Text=“Please, call me May.”
#Text=She moved into the kitchen and held out a box of orange juice.
#Text=“You want some?”
#Text=“Uh, sure.”
#Text=Bucky sat at the stool on the other side of the counter and stared at the woman, trying to get a read on her.
#Text=He’d read about her in the kid’s file.
#Text=She was a graphic designer, worked for some consulting firm uptown.
#Text=The art books, her trendy dress, and wire-frame glasses - it all added up nicely.
#Text=What did
#Text=not
#Text=quite fit, however, was how she knew the kid’s secret.
#Text=How she was alright with the kid’s dangerous alter-ego.
#Text=Bucky accepted the orange juice and drank it out of politeness.
#Text=There was too much pulp for his taste - the mush stuck in the spaces between his teeth.
#Text=“Thanks,” he said.
#Text=He had a long time to figure out the nuance in her relationship and attitude toward the kid.
#Text=He wasn’t about to blow the good terms between himself and the kid by pushing the Aunt before she was ready to speak.
#Text=“So,” she said, looking down her nose at Bucky.
#Text=“You’re the one who’s ‘taking care’ of Peter.”
#Text=“Yes, ma- May.”
#Text=“Hmm.”
#Text=She packed a brown lunch bag, apparently disinterested in Bucky.
#Text=It was a feint, he knew, but he played along.
#Text=“Well, it can’t hurt to have someone watching his back.”
#Text=“I agree.”
#Text=Bucky set down his empty glass.
#Text=Luckily, he was spared from more awkward small talk.
#Text=The kid practically sprang out of the bathroom, his hair wet and slicked over, wearing a blue plaid shirt.
#Text=“May - I thought you were at work.”
#Text=She smiled at him.
#Text=There was a hint of a ‘we’re going to talk about this later’ look in her eyes.
#Text=“I couldn’t miss you on your first day of senior year.”
#Text=She handed him the bag.
#Text=“There are so many exciting things ahead for you, Pete.”
#Text=She pulled the kid in and squeezed him in a hug.
#Text=The kid squeezed back.
#Text=“Thanks, May.”
#Text=Bucky stared at the floors boards.
#Text=The wood had a nice stain.
#Text=After a moment, the two finished up their good-byes and promised to check in with each other throughout the day.
#Text=“Have a good day, James,” she said to Bucky.
#Text=Her eyes, he decided, where dangerous.
#Text=Like Natasha’s when she had a whole plan worked out in her head.
#Text=“Thanks.
#Text=You - you too.”
#Text=He walked out of the apartment with the kid.
#Text=“Ready for your first day?”
#Text=The kid shrugged.
#Text=“As I’ll ever be.”
#Text=Chapter 6
#Text=To Bucky’s surprise, the kid didn’t slide into the passenger side of the car.
#Text=Instead, he threw himself down in the backseat, directly behind Bucky, tossed his backpack next to him, and clicked in his seatbelt.
#Text=Bucky said nothing.
#Text=He adjusted the rear-view mirror, bringing it lower until the kid’s whole head was visible in the glass.
#Text=Bucky put the car in reverse, backed out of the space, and set off on the route to the kid’s school in midtown.
#Text=“Um, Mr.
#Text=Barnes?”
#Text=Bucky drummed his fingers against the wheel.
#Text=The rush hour traffic slowed to a crawl.
#Text=He pulled out from behind a slow blue truck and cut through a parking lot, before rejoining the flow of traffic on the other side.
#Text=“Yeah?”
#Text=The kid looked a bit pale at Bucky’s maneuver.
#Text=“You should wear your seatbelt.”
#Text=In the mirror, Bucky met the kid’s eyes.
#Text=“My
#Text=seatbelt?”
#Text=He nodded weakly.
#Text=“Uh, yeah.
#Text=Yeah.”
#Text=Bucky rode the tail of a white delivery van.
#Text=“Kid.”
#Text=He whipped around the van.
#Text=“Are you serious?”
#Text=“Well - yeah.”
#Text=The kid was something else.
#Text=One hand on the wheel, Bucky grabbed the seatbelt and tugged it roughly over his shoulder.
#Text=He jammed it into the buckle.
#Text=“Happy?”
#Text=“Very.
#Text=Seatbelts save lives, you know.”
#Text=Bucky shook his head.
#Text=The kid was annoying as all hell, but at least he kept quiet for the rest of the ride.
#Text=Bucky pulled the car to the curb in front of Midtown Science and Technology.
#Text=“Right,” he said as the kid opened the door.
#Text=“I’ll be back here at 3:30.
#Text=Don’t be late.”
#Text=“Alright.
#Text=Have a good day, Mr.
#Text=Barnes.”
#Text=“You too, kid.”
#Text=Bucky watched as the kid joined a throng of kids and walked inside.
#Text=Instead of heading back to his place, Bucky parked around the side of the street and went for a stroll.
#Text=He studied the streets, memorizing the alleys and shops and subway entrances.
#Text=A decent fenced lined the grounds of the school - all things considered, the place was pretty safe: a limited number of exits and plenty of security cameras in the surrounding streets.
#Text=The kid would be alright.
#Text=Over the next week, Bucky and the kid continued in their pattern.
#Text=In the morning, they’d jog.
#Text=Bucky would take the kid to school and pick him up at the end of the day.
#Text=At night, he sat in front of his laptop and watched the kid’s red blinking light cross over the grid of the city.
#Text=When Bucky wasn’t with the kid, he tried to take in as much of the city as he could.
#Text=The sweltering heat of summer gave way to the fresh September air, and he no longer felt as out of place in the long-sleeved shirts and sweaters he wore to cover his arm.
#Text=It became easier to blend into the crowd, easier to remain unnoticed.
#Text=There was still so much of the city that was foreign to him.
#Text=New buildings everywhere.
#Text=Some of the places Bucky remembered were more or less untouched.
#Text=Others, like Brooklyn and Hell’s Kitchen, had morphed from places for working-class immigrants to trendy areas with unimaginable rent.
#Text=His head still ached.
#Text=The nightmares still came.
#Text=Despite it all, the world was becoming more manageable.
#Text=Someday, Bucky imagined he might even be able to live in it without being haunted by the ghosts of his memory.
#Text=On Friday afternoon, Bucky waited for the kid, as was becoming their routine.
#Text=The time slowly ticked past 3:30, rolling into 3:45.
#Text=Bucky tensed his hand around the steering wheel.
#Text=The kid said Bucky couldn’t interrupt, and according to his location, he was still inside the building.
#Text=As the clocked inched closer to 4, Bucky stepped out of the car and slammed the door behind him.
#Text=He entered the school and took in the halls.
#Text=Images of scientists and inventors lined the walls, and Bucky couldn’t help but feeling a little jealous of the place.
#Text=He would’ve given everything to go to a school like this when he was young.
#Text=“Can I help you?”
#Text=A young woman in a frilled pink blouse poked her head out of the office.
#Text=“I’m here to pick up my nephew,” Bucky lied.
#Text=“He’s late.”
#Text=The woman gestured to Bucky to come into the office, so Bucky followed.
#Text=She sat behind the main computer.
#Text=“What’s your nephew’s name?”
#Text=“Peter Parker.
#Text=He’s a senior.”
#Text=The woman nodded paged the kid to the main office over the intercom speaker and then turned to her computer, typing away.
#Text=“We’re still updating some files,” she explained.
#Text=“After, you know, everything.”
#Text=She waved her hand vaguely around as if that explained the horror.
#Text=“It’s been a real mess, I’ll tell you that much.
#Text=And the old secretary, the one in the job before me, she wants the job back.
#Text=Waiting for me to fail so she can swoop in and take my place.
#Text=But I mean it’s not
#Text=my
#Text=fault she disappeared.”
#Text=Bucky forced the corners of his mouth up in a smile.
#Text=“No, it’s not.”
#Text=As it turned out, Bucky didn’t need to worry with fake peasantry - the woman was absorbed in her screen.
#Text=“Ah, here we go, Peter Parker.
#Text=Right now the only contact we have for him is May Parker, his aunt.
#Text=I’m assuming you’re her husband?”
#Text=Bucky chuckled.
#Text=“No, actually.
#Text=May’s a relative on his father’s side.
#Text=I’m on his mother’s.”
#Text=The woman nodded, her eyes perking up at Bucky’s relationship status.
#Text=Her eyes flashed toward his hand, likely searching for a ring, but Bucky kept his left hand buried in his pocket.
#Text=“I’ll need your name and phone number for the contact information," she said.
